items.title: Contributing
items.text: Found a bug or have a feature request? Open an issue or discussion on GitHub.

items.title: FAQ
items.text: Frequently asked questions about fmd.

items.title: What is fmd?
items.text: fmd (Frappe Manager Deployer) is a zero-downtime deployment tool for Frappe applications. It provides atomic releases, instant rollback, and automated migrations.

items.title: How does fmd relate to Frappe Manager?
items.text:
fmd works alongside Frappe Manager (fm):

  • Frappe Manager: Creates and manages Frappe benches in Docker
  • fmd: Handles deployments with versioned releases on top of FM benches

You need both: FM for the runtime environment, fmd for deployment automation.

items.title: What's the difference between FM and fmd?
items.text: Feature Frappe Manager (fm) fmd Purpose Development environment Production deployment Scope Bench creation, Docker management Release management, migrations, rollback Target Developers DevOps, CI/CD pipelines Deployment Single bench state Versioned releases with history

items.title: Can I use fmd without Frappe Manager?
items.text: No. fmd requires a Frappe Manager bench as the foundation. It extends FM with production deployment capabilities.

items.title: Which Python version do I need?
items.text: Python 3.10 or later. fmd is tested on Python 3.10, 3.11, 3.12, and 3.13.

items.title: Do I need to install Docker?
items.text: Yes. fmd uses Docker containers (via Frappe Manager) to build and run Frappe applications.

items.title: Can I use fmd on Windows?
items.text: fmd is designed for Linux and macOS. For Windows, use WSL 2 (Windows Subsystem for Linux).

items.title: How do I access private GitHub repositories?
items.text:
Set a GitHub personal access token:

export GITHUB_TOKEN=ghp_your_token_here

Or add to your config file:

github_token = "ghp_your_token_here"

items.title: What's the difference between pull and ship mode?
items.text:
• Pull: Build on production server, deploy locally (simpler, uses server resources)
  • Ship: Build locally/CI, rsync to remote (offloads builds, enables parallel deploys)

See Deploy Modes Guide for details.

items.title: How long does a deployment take?
items.text:
First deployment: 8-15 minutes (downloads dependencies, builds apps)

Subsequent deployments: 3-6 minutes (uses cache, only rebuilds changed apps)

Actual time depends on:

  • Number of apps
  • Server resources (CPU, RAM, network)
  • Cache hit rate

items.title: Can I deploy to multiple servers at once?
items.text:
Yes, with ship mode:

fmd deploy ship --config server1.toml
fmd deploy ship --config server2.toml
fmd deploy ship --config server3.toml

Build once, ship to multiple servers.

items.title: Will deployment cause downtime?
items.text:
Minimal (typically <1 second) during symlink switch.

Enable maintenance_mode = true for user-friendly maintenance page during migrations.

items.title: What happens if deployment fails?
items.text:
• If rollback = true: Automatically reverts to previous release
  • If rollback = false: Site stays on previous working release, new release is kept for debugging

Either way, your site stays up.

items.title: How many releases should I keep?
items.text:
Default is 7. This balances:

  • Disk space: Each release ~700 MB (Frappe + ERPNext)
  • Rollback options: 7 releases = ~1-2 weeks of history

Adjust with:

[release]
releases_retain_limit = 7

items.title: Can I rollback to any previous release?
items.text:
Yes, as long as the release still exists (not cleaned up):

fmd release list mysite.localhost
fmd release switch mysite.localhost release_YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS

items.title: What happens to old releases?
items.text:
When you exceed releases_retain_limit, fmd automatically:

  1. Backs up old releases to deployment-backup/
  2. Deletes old releases from workspace

items.title: Can I manually delete releases?
items.text:
Yes:

fmd cleanup mysite.localhost -r 3 -y

Keeps 3 most recent releases, deletes the rest.

items.title: Can I sync from Frappe Cloud?
items.text:
Yes. fmd can import:

  • App list and commit hashes
  • Python version
  • Database backups

See Frappe Cloud Sync Guide.

items.title: Does fmd work with Frappe Cloud?
items.text:
fmd is for self-hosted deployments. It can sync from Frappe Cloud but doesn't deploy to Frappe Cloud.

Use case: Migrate from FC to self-hosted infrastructure.

items.title: Do I need Frappe Cloud credentials?
items.text: Only if you want to sync from FC. For standard deployments, no FC account needed.

items.title: Build fails with "GitHub API rate limit exceeded"
items.text:
Set a GitHub token:

export GITHUB_TOKEN=ghp_your_token_here

This increases rate limit from 60 to 5000 requests/hour.

items.title: Migration fails during deployment
items.text:
Check migration logs:

cat ~/frappe/sites/mysite/workspace/frappe-bench/.migrate_log

If rollback enabled, site automatically reverts to previous release.

items.title: "Permission denied" errors
items.text: Never use sudo with fmd or pip. Use uv tool or pipx instead (user-space installation).

items.title: Site not accessible after deployment
items.text:
Check bench status:

fm info mysite

If stopped:

fm start mysite

items.title: How do I enable debug logging?
items.text:
fmd -v deploy pull --config site.toml

The -v flag must come before the subcommand.

items.title: Can I customize the build process?
items.text:
Yes, use hooks. Example:

[[apps]]
repo = "my-org/my-app"
ref = "main"
before_bench_build = """
npm ci
npm run build:prod
"""

See Hooks Guide for details.

items.title: Can I use monorepo apps?
items.text:
Yes:

[[apps]]
repo = "my-org/monorepo"
ref = "main"
subdir_path = "apps/my-app"

See Monorepo Apps Guide.

items.title: Can I run workers on separate servers?
items.text:
Yes, use remote workers:

fmd remote-worker enable mysite.localhost --rw-server 192.168.1.100

See Remote Workers Guide.

items.title: How do I change site domain?
items.text:
Use search-replace:

fmd search-replace mysite.localhost "old.com" "new.com" --dry-run
fmd search-replace mysite.localhost "old.com" "new.com"

Then update site config and nginx.

items.title: Can I deploy custom branches?
items.text:
Yes:

fmd deploy pull mysite.localhost --app frappe/frappe:my-feature-branch

Or in config:

[[apps]]
repo = "frappe/frappe"
ref = "my-feature-branch"

items.title: Where can I get support?
items.text:
• Documentation: https://rtcamp.github.io/fmd/
  • GitHub Issues: https://github.com/rtcamp/fmd/issues
  • Discussions: https://github.com/rtcamp/fmd/discussions

items.title: How do I report a bug?
items.text:
Open an issue: https://github.com/rtcamp/fmd/issues/new

Include:

  • fmd version (fmd --version)
  • Full command and output
  • Relevant logs
  • Config file (remove secrets)

items.title: How do I request a feature?
items.text: Start a discussion: https://github.com/rtcamp/fmd/discussions/new

items.title: Is fmd production-ready?
items.text: fmd is actively developed and used in production by rtCamp. However, always test deployments in staging first.

items.title: Command Reference
items.text: Complete reference for all fmd CLI commands. Each command page includes usage, options, and real-world examples.

items.title: Quick Start
items.text:
The most common commands to get you started:

  • Deploy

    fmd deploy pull --config site.toml
fmd deploy ship --config site.toml

  • Release Management

    fmd release configure mysite.localhost
fmd release create mysite.localhost
fmd release switch mysite.localhost release_YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS

  • Info

    fmd info mysite.localhost
fmd release list mysite.localhost

  • Cleanup

    fmd cleanup mysite.localhost -r 3 -b 5 -y

items.title: Deploy Commands
items.text: Full automated deployment workflows.

items.title: fmd deploy
items.text:
Full automated deployment: configure → create → switch

Two deployment strategies:

  • fmd deploy pull — Build on production server, deploy locally
  • fmd deploy ship — Build locally/CI, ship to remote server
fmd deploy pull --config site.toml
fmd deploy ship --config site.toml

items.title: Release Commands
items.text: Manual control over release lifecycle for advanced workflows.

items.title: fmd release configure
items.text:
One-time workspace setup

Converts a plain Frappe Manager bench into a versioned release structure.

fmd release configure mysite.localhost

items.title: fmd release create
items.text:
Build a new release

Creates a new timestamped release without activating it. Safe operation — no changes to live site.

fmd release create mysite.localhost
fmd release create --config site.toml

items.title: fmd release switch
items.text:
Activate a release

Atomically switch to a specific release. This is where migrations run and the site goes live with new code.

fmd release switch mysite.localhost release_20260416_143022

items.title: fmd release list
items.text:
Show all releases

List all releases for a site with timestamps and status.

fmd release list mysite.localhost

items.title: fmd info
items.text:
Show site details

Display current release, installed apps, configuration, and deployment status.

fmd info mysite.localhost

items.title: fmd cleanup
items.text:
Clean up old releases and backups

Remove old releases and backups to free disk space.

fmd cleanup mysite.localhost -r 3 -b 5 -y

items.title: fmd search-replace
items.text:
Search and replace in database

Bulk find-and-replace operations across site database. Useful for domain changes.

fmd search-replace mysite.localhost "old.com" "new.com" --dry-run

items.title: fmd maintenance
items.text:
Manage maintenance mode

Enable/disable maintenance mode and generate bypass tokens.

fmd maintenance enable mysite.localhost
fmd maintenance disable mysite.localhost

items.title: fmd remote-worker
items.text:
Manage remote workers

Configure and sync releases to remote worker servers.

fmd remote-worker enable mysite.localhost --rw-server 192.168.1.100
fmd remote-worker sync mysite.localhost --rw-server 192.168.1.100

Quick Help

Use fmd <command> --help to see detailed options and examples for any command.

Global Options

  • fmd -v <command> — Enable verbose logging (must precede subcommand)
  • -c, --config <path> — Load TOML config file (accepted by all commands)

items.title: Command Reference
items.text:
Complete reference for all fmd commands.

Global option: fmd -v <command> enables verbose logging (must precede subcommand).

Config option: All commands accept --config / -c <path> to load a TOML config file.

items.title: fmd deploy pull
items.text:
Full automated deployment: configure (if needed) → create release → switch.

Synopsis: 

fmd deploy pull [OPTIONS] [BENCH_NAME]

Options: - -c, --config <path> — TOML config file - --app / -a <spec> — App to deploy (format: org/repo:ref or org/repo:ref:subdir) - --maintenance-mode — Enable maintenance mode during switch - --no-maintenance-mode — Disable maintenance mode - --backups — Take DB backup before switch - --no-backups — Skip DB backup - --rollback — Auto-rollback on failure - --no-rollback — Don't auto-rollback - --python-version <ver> — Python version (e.g., 3.11) - --github-token <token> — GitHub token for private repos - --fc-key, --fc-secret, --fc-site, --fc-team — Frappe Cloud credentials - --fc-use-apps — Import app list from FC - --fc-use-deps — Import python_version from FC - --fc-use-db — Download and restore FC backup at switch time

Examples: 

# Minimal (uses config file)
fmd deploy pull --config site.toml

# With bench name (no config)
fmd deploy pull site.localhost

# Full with overrides
fmd deploy pull site.localhost \
  --app frappe/frappe:version-15 \
  --app frappe/erpnext:version-15 \
  --maintenance-mode --backups --rollback

# Monorepo subdirectory app
fmd deploy pull --config site.toml \
  --app my-org/monorepo:main:apps/my-app

# With Frappe Cloud integration
fmd deploy pull --config site.toml \
  --fc-key <key> --fc-secret <secret> --fc-site mysite.frappe.cloud \
  --fc-use-apps --fc-use-deps --fc-use-db

Workflow: 1. Check if workspace is configured; if not, run release configure 2. Create new release with release create 3. Switch to new release with release switch

items.title: fmd deploy ship
items.text:
Build release locally → rsync to remote server → switch on remote.

Synopsis: 

fmd deploy ship --config <path> [OPTIONS]

Important: --config is required for ship mode (must specify [ship] section).

Options: - -c, --config <path> — TOML config file (required) - --app / -a <spec> — App to deploy - --maintenance-mode — Enable maintenance mode on remote - --backups — Take DB backup on remote before switch - All other options from deploy pull

Config required: 

[ship]
server_ip = "192.168.1.100"     # Remote server
ssh_user = "frappe"             # SSH username
ssh_port = 22                   # SSH port
remote_workspace_root = "/home/frappe/frappe/sites/site.localhost/workspace"

Example: 

fmd deploy ship --config site.toml --app frappe/frappe:version-15

Workflow: 1. Build release locally using Docker (no FM services needed) 2. rsync release directory to remote server 3. SSH to remote, run fmd release switch to activate 4. Run post-switch hooks on remote

items.title: Release Commands
items.text: Low-level primitives for manual release management and CI/CD pipelines.

items.title: fmd release configure
items.text:
One-time workspace setup. Converts a plain bench into a versioned release structure.

Synopsis: 

fmd release configure [OPTIONS] [BENCH_NAME]

Options: - -c, --config <path> — TOML config file - --app / -a <spec> — Initial apps to configure - --backups — Enable backups by default

Example: 

fmd release configure site.localhost
fmd release configure --config site.toml

What it creates: - deployment-data/ — Persistent data directory - sites/ — Site DB and files - config/ — Supervisor configs - logs/ — Application logs - deployment-backup/ — Backup storage - release_YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS/ — First release directory

Next step: Run fmd release create to build a new release, or fmd deploy pull for automated deploy.

items.title: fmd release create
items.text:
Create a new release without affecting the live bench.

Synopsis: 

fmd release create [OPTIONS] [BENCH_NAME]

Options: - -c, --config <path> — TOML config file - --app / -a <spec> — Apps to include - --python-version <ver> — Python version - --node-version <ver> — Node.js version - --mode <exec|image> — Runner mode (default: exec) - --build-dir <path> — Output directory for image mode (forces image mode) - --runner-image <image> — Docker image for image mode

Modes:

  • exec (default): Build using Frappe Manager's running docker-compose services. Fast, requires FM site to be running.
  • image: Build in a fresh Docker container. Slower, but works without FM running. Required for CI/CD.

Examples: 

# Standard create (uses running FM services)
fmd release create site.localhost

# With config file
fmd release create --config site.toml

# CI/CD mode: build in isolated container
fmd release create --mode image --build-dir /tmp/releases
# Output: /tmp/releases/release_YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS/

# Use custom Docker image
fmd release create --mode image --runner-image ghcr.io/my-org/fm-builder:latest

What it does: 1. Clone app repositories (or update if symlinked) 2. Create Python venv with release-scoped .uv/ cache 3. Install Python dependencies 4. Install Node.js in release-scoped .fnm/ 5. Run bench build for frontend assets 6. Execute pre/post-build hooks

Safe: Does not touch frappe-bench symlink. Can run while site is live.

items.title: fmd release switch
items.text:
Atomically activate a previously-created release.

Synopsis: 

fmd release switch [OPTIONS] BENCH_NAME RELEASE_NAME

Arguments: - BENCH_NAME — Bench/site name - RELEASE_NAME — Release directory name (e.g., release_20250410_120000)

Options: - -c, --config <path> — TOML config file - --maintenance-mode — Enable maintenance mode during switch - --backups — Take DB backup before switch - --rollback — Auto-rollback on failure - --migrate / --no-migrate — Run bench migrate (default: yes) - --search-replace / --no-search-replace — Run DB search-replace (default: yes)

Examples: 

# Minimal
fmd release switch site.localhost release_20250410_120000

# With safety features
fmd release switch site.localhost release_20250410_120000 \
  --maintenance-mode --backups --rollback

# Rollback to previous release
fmd release switch site.localhost release_20250409_100000

Workflow: 1. Validate release exists and is ready 2. Drain workers (if drain_workers = true) 3. Take DB backup (if backups = true) 4. Enable maintenance mode (during configured phases only) 5. Update frappe-bench symlink → release_YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS/ (atomic) 6. Patch common_site_config.json and site_config.json (if configured) 7. Run bench migrate 8. Restart services (fmx restart) 9. Disable maintenance mode 10. Sync to remote workers (if sync_workers = true)

Downtime: Typically <30s (migrate + restart only). Symlink switch is instant.

items.title: fmd release list
items.text:
List all releases with current marker and metadata.

Synopsis: 

fmd release list [OPTIONS] [BENCH_NAME]

Options: - -c, --config <path> — TOML config file

Example: 

fmd release list site.localhost

Output: 

╭──────────────────────────────┬──────────┬────────┬──────┬──────┬──────────────────────────────────────────╮
│ Status │ Release               │ Size     │ Python │ Node │ Apps │ Symlink                                  │
├────────┼───────────────────────┼──────────┼────────┼──────┼──────┼──────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ ●      │ release_20250410_1200 │ 1.2 GB   │ 3.11   │ 18   │ 2    │ workspace/frappe-bench → (current)       │
│        │ release_20250409_1000 │ 1.1 GB   │ 3.11   │ 18   │ 2    │ workspace/frappe-bench-prev              │
│        │ release_20250408_0900 │ 1.0 GB   │ 3.10   │ 16   │ 2    │ (no symlink)                             │
╰────────┴───────────────────────┴──────────┴────────┴──────┴──────┴──────────────────────────────────────────╯

  • ● indicates the currently active release (symlinked via frappe-bench)

items.title: fmd cleanup
items.text:
Remove old releases and backups based on retention policy.

Synopsis: 

fmd cleanup [OPTIONS] [BENCH_NAME]

Options: - -c, --config <path> — TOML config file - -r, --release-retain-limit <n> — Keep last N releases (default: 7) - -b, --backup-retain-limit <n> — Keep last N backups (default: same as releases) - --show-sizes — Display disk usage for each item - -y, --yes — Skip confirmation prompt

Examples: 

# Interactive (prompts for confirmation)
fmd cleanup site.localhost

# Auto-confirm, keep 3 releases and 5 backups
fmd cleanup site.localhost -r 3 -b 5 -y

# Show sizes before deleting
fmd cleanup --config site.toml --show-sizes

Workflow: 1. List all releases and backups 2. Mark oldest items for deletion (beyond retention limit) 3. Show what will be deleted (with sizes if --show-sizes) 4. Prompt for confirmation (unless -y) 5. Delete marked items

Safety: Never deletes the currently active release (marked with ●).

items.title: fmd search-replace
items.text:
Find and replace text across all text fields in the Frappe database.

Synopsis: 

fmd search-replace [OPTIONS] BENCH_NAME SEARCH REPLACE

Arguments: - BENCH_NAME — Bench/site name - SEARCH — Text to find - REPLACE — Replacement text

Options: - -c, --config <path> — TOML config file - --dry-run — Show what would be replaced without making changes

Examples: 

# Dry run (preview changes)
fmd search-replace site.localhost "old.domain.com" "new.domain.com" --dry-run

# Execute replacement
fmd search-replace site.localhost "old.domain.com" "new.domain.com"

# Using config file
fmd search-replace --config site.toml "old-text" "new-text"

Use cases: - Domain migration: Change all URLs after moving to a new domain - Text corrections: Fix typos in DB content - Configuration updates: Update hardcoded values across the DB

Limitations: Only works in FM mode (uses bench --site all search-replace internally).

items.title: fmd info
items.text:
Show git state for each installed app.

Synopsis: 

fmd info [OPTIONS] [BENCH_NAME]

Options: - -c, --config <path> — TOML config file

Example: 

fmd info site.localhost

Output: 

[[apps]]
app_name = "frappe"
repo = "frappe/frappe"
ref = "a1b2c3d4e5"  # Commit hash
branch = "version-15"
tag = ""
latest_commit = "fix: resolve caching issue in background jobs"

[[apps]]
app_name = "erpnext"
repo = "frappe/erpnext"
ref = "f9e8d7c6b5"
branch = "version-15"
tag = "v15.1.0"
latest_commit = "feat: add support for multi-currency invoices"

items.title: fmd remote-worker enable
items.text:
Enable remote worker mode by opening database and cache ports in docker-compose.

Synopsis: 

fmd remote-worker enable [OPTIONS] BENCH_NAME

Options: - -c, --config <path> — TOML config file - --rw-server <ip> — Remote worker server IP - --rw-user <user> — SSH username for remote (default: frappe) - --force — Skip confirmation prompts

Example: 

fmd remote-worker enable site.localhost --rw-server 192.168.1.100 --force

What it does: 1. Scans for available ports: - Redis: 11000+ - MariaDB: 3306+ (finds first free port) 2. Updates docker-compose.override.yml to expose ports: 

services:
  mariadb:
    ports:
      - "3307:3306"
  redis-cache:
    ports:
      - "11000:6379"
  redis-queue:
    ports:
      - "11001:6379"
3. Writes connection details to workspace/.remote-worker-config.json 4. Restarts docker-compose services

Network requirements: - Firewall must allow connections on the exposed ports - Remote worker must be able to reach primary server IP

items.title: fmd remote-worker sync
items.text:
Rsync release directory to remote worker server.

Synopsis: 

fmd remote-worker sync [OPTIONS] BENCH_NAME

Options: - -c, --config <path> — TOML config file - --rw-server <ip> — Remote worker server IP - --rw-user <user> — SSH username (default: frappe)

Example: 

fmd remote-worker sync site.localhost --rw-server 192.168.1.100

What gets synced: - apps/ — Application code - env/ — Python virtual environment - .uv/, .fnm/ — Runtime caches - Config files

Excluded (uses remote server's own): - sites/ — Remote has its own DB/files - public/ — Generated on remote - private/ — Site-specific files - .git/, node_modules/, __pycache__/

Workflow: 1. Read .remote-worker-config.json for remote connection details 2. rsync current release directory to remote server 3. SSH to remote and restart services

Performance: First sync is slow (full copy). Subsequent syncs are incremental (fast).

items.title: Global Options
items.text:
All commands accept:

  • --config / -c <path> — TOML config file (alternative to positional BENCH_NAME)
  • -v / --verbose — Enable verbose logging (must precede subcommand: fmd -v deploy pull)

Config priority: 1. CLI arguments (highest) 2. Environment variables 3. TOML config file 4. Built-in defaults (lowest)

Exit codes: - 0 — Success - 1 — Error (logged to stderr) - 2 — Validation error (invalid arguments or config)

items.title: Getting Started
items.text:
Everything you need to go from zero to deploying Frappe sites with fmd.

  • Requirements

    Check what you need before installing — Python 3.10+, Docker, Frappe Manager, and a few minutes.

  • Installation

    Install fmd with uv, pipx, or from source. Configure GitHub tokens for private repositories.

  • Quick Start

    Deploy your first Frappe site step by step. Learn the most common deployment workflows and commands.

items.title: Installation
items.text: Install fmd using your preferred Python package manager.

items.title: Using uv (Recommended)
items.text: uv is a fast Python package installer and resolver.

items.title: Install as a tool
items.text:
uv tool install frappe-deployer

This installs fmd globally and makes the fmd command available.

items.title: Run without installing
items.text:
uvx frappe-deployer --help

This runs fmd directly from PyPI without permanent installation.

items.title: Install development version
items.text: uv tool install git+https://github.com/rtcamp/fmd@main

items.title: Upgrade
items.text: uv tool upgrade frappe-deployer

items.title: Install
items.text: pipx install frappe-deployer

items.title: Install development version
items.text: pipx install git+https://github.com/rtcamp/fmd@main

items.title: Upgrade
items.text: pipx upgrade frappe-deployer

items.title: Clone the repository
items.text:
git clone https://github.com/rtcamp/fmd.git
cd fmd

items.title: Install in development mode
items.text:
pip install -e .

This creates an editable installation — changes to the source code take effect immediately.

items.title: Install with uv
items.text: uv pip install -e .

items.title: Verify Installation
items.text:
Check that fmd is installed correctly:

fmd --version

You should see output like:

fmd version 0.1.0

Check available commands:

fmd --help

items.title: GitHub Token
items.text:
Set up your GitHub token for private repositories:

export GITHUB_TOKEN=ghp_your_token_here

Add to your shell profile (~/.bashrc, ~/.zshrc, etc.) to persist:

echo 'export GITHUB_TOKEN=ghp_your_token_here' >> ~/.bashrc

Alternatively, add it to your config file (see Configuration Guide).

items.title: Frappe Cloud (optional)
items.text:
If you plan to sync from Frappe Cloud, set up credentials:

export FC_API_KEY=your_api_key
export FC_API_SECRET=your_api_secret

Or add to your config file:

[fc]
api_key = "your_api_key"
api_secret = "your_api_secret"
site_name = "yoursite.frappe.cloud"
team_name = "your-team"

items.title: Command not found
items.text:
If fmd command is not found after installation:

uv/pipx: Ensure the tool installation directory is in your PATH:

echo 'export PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH"' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc

From source: Ensure you're in the correct virtual environment or the installation directory is in your PATH.

items.title: Permission denied
items.text:
If you get permission errors during installation:

Never use sudo with pip. Use uv tool or pipx instead, which install in user space.

If you must use pip directly:

pip install --user frappe-deployer

items.title: Network issues behind proxy
items.text:
Configure proxy settings:

export HTTP_PROXY=http://proxy.example.com:8080
export HTTPS_PROXY=http://proxy.example.com:8080
uv tool install frappe-deployer

items.title: Next Steps
items.text: Now that fmd is installed, proceed to the Quick Start guide to deploy your first site.

items.title: Quick Start
items.text: This guide walks you through your first deployment with fmd — from zero to a running Frappe site.

items.title: Prerequisites
items.text:
Make sure you have:

  • Python 3.10+ installed
  • Docker and Docker Compose V2 running
  • Frappe Manager installed (fm --version works)
  • fmd installed (fmd --version works)

If not, see Requirements and Installation.

items.title: Step 1: Create a Frappe Manager Bench
items.text:
First, create a bench using Frappe Manager. This sets up the Docker environment where your site will run.

fm create mysite --environment prod

This creates a production bench named mysite. The bench directory is created at:

~/frappe/sites/mysite/

Development vs Production

Use --environment dev for development benches with developer tools enabled.

items.title: Step 2: Configure Your Deployment Workspace
items.text:
Navigate to your bench directory and configure the fmd workspace:

cd ~/frappe/sites/mysite
fmd release configure mysite.localhost

This creates the workspace structure:

~/frappe/sites/mysite/workspace/
├── .cache/              # Build cache
└── deployment-data/     # Persistent data (sites, logs, config)

items.title: Step 3: Deploy Frappe + ERPNext
items.text:
Now deploy your apps with a single command:

fmd deploy pull mysite.localhost \
  --app frappe/frappe:version-15 \
  --app frappe/erpnext:version-15 \
  --maintenance-mode \
  --backups

What happens:

  1. Creates a new timestamped release (e.g., release_20260416_143022)
  2. Clones app repositories from GitHub
  3. Builds Frappe bench inside Docker container
  4. Enables maintenance mode
  5. Takes database backup
  6. Runs migrations
  7. Switches to the new release atomically (symlink update)
  8. Restarts services
  9. Disables maintenance mode

This typically takes 5-15 minutes depending on your internet speed and server resources.

items.title: Step 4: Verify Deployment
items.text:
Check that your site is running:

fmd info mysite.localhost

You should see output showing:

  • Current release (e.g., release_20260416_143022)
  • Installed apps (frappe, erpnext)
  • Site status

List all releases:

fmd release list mysite.localhost

items.title: Step 5: Access Your Site
items.text:
Your site is now live! Access it at:

http://mysite.localhost

Default credentials:

  • Username: Administrator
  • Password: admin (or the password you set during bench creation)

items.title: Using a Configuration File
items.text:
For repeated deployments, use a config file instead of command-line flags.

Create site.toml:

site_name = "mysite.localhost"
bench_name = "mysite"
github_token = "ghp_your_token"  # Optional, for private repos

[[apps]]
repo = "frappe/frappe"
ref = "version-15"

[[apps]]
repo = "frappe/erpnext"
ref = "version-15"

[switch]
migrate = true
maintenance_mode = true
backups = true

Then deploy with:

fmd deploy pull --config site.toml

Much cleaner! See Configuration Guide for all available options.

items.title: Update Apps (New Deployment)
items.text:
To deploy updates (e.g., new app versions or code changes):

fmd deploy pull --config site.toml

This creates a new release with the latest code and switches to it.

items.title: Rollback to Previous Release
items.text:
If something goes wrong, instantly rollback:

fmd release list mysite.localhost
# Find the previous release timestamp
fmd release switch mysite.localhost release_20260415_120000

items.title: Deploy with Custom Python Version
items.text:
fmd deploy pull mysite.localhost \
  --app frappe/frappe:version-15 \
  --python-version 3.11

items.title: Deploy a Monorepo App
items.text:
For apps in subdirectories of a monorepo:

fmd deploy pull mysite.localhost \
  --app my-org/monorepo:main:apps/my-app

Format: org/repo:ref:subdir_path

items.title: Next Steps
items.text:
Now that you've deployed your first site, explore:

  • Deploy Modes — Learn about pull vs ship deployment strategies
  • GitHub Actions — Automate deployments from CI/CD
  • Hooks — Customize build and deployment lifecycle
  • Frappe Cloud Sync — Sync apps and databases from Frappe Cloud
  • Configuration — Deep dive into all config options

items.title: Build fails during deployment
items.text:
Check logs:

fm logs mysite

Common issues:

  • GitHub rate limit: Set GITHUB_TOKEN environment variable
  • Network timeout: Increase timeout or check internet connection
  • Disk space: Ensure at least 5 GB free space

items.title: Site not accessible
items.text:
Verify the bench is running:

fm info mysite

If stopped, start it:

fm start mysite

items.title: Migration errors
items.text:
If migrations fail, fmd auto-rollbacks to the previous release (if rollback = true in config).

Check which release is active:

fmd info mysite.localhost

Manually switch if needed:

fmd release switch mysite.localhost release_YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS

For more troubleshooting, see Troubleshooting Guide.

items.title: Requirements
items.text: Before installing fmd, make sure you have the following:

items.title: Python 3.10+
items.text:
fmd requires Python 3.10 or later.

Check your Python version:

python3 --version

If you need to install or upgrade Python:

macOS (Homebrew)Ubuntu/DebianRHEL/CentOS/Rocky 
brew install python@3.11

sudo apt update
sudo apt install python3.11 python3.11-venv python3-pip

sudo dnf install python3.11

items.title: Docker
items.text:
Docker is required for building and running Frappe containers.

Check if Docker is installed:

docker --version
docker compose version

If you need to install Docker:

  • Linux: Follow the official Docker installation guide
  • macOS: Install Docker Desktop for Mac
  • Windows: Install Docker Desktop for Windows

Docker Compose V2

fmd requires Docker Compose V2 (the docker compose command, not the legacy docker-compose).

items.title: Frappe Manager
items.text:
fmd works alongside Frappe Manager to manage Frappe benches.

Install Frappe Manager:

uv tool install --python 3.13 frappe-manager

Or with pipx:

pipx install frappe-manager

Verify installation:

fm --version

items.title: GitHub Personal Access Token
items.text:
Required for:

  • Deploying private repositories
  • Avoiding GitHub API rate limits

Create a token at github.com/settings/tokens with repo scope.

Store it securely:

export GITHUB_TOKEN=ghp_your_token_here

Or add to your config file:

github_token = "ghp_your_token_here"

items.title: SSH Access (for remote deployments)
items.text:
If you plan to use fmd deploy ship or remote workers, you'll need:

  • SSH access to your production server
  • Passwordless SSH key authentication configured

Test SSH access:

ssh user@your-server.com

items.title: Frappe Cloud Credentials (optional)
items.text:
If you want to sync from Frappe Cloud, you'll need:

  • Frappe Cloud API key and secret
  • Team name and site name

Get these from your Frappe Cloud dashboard.

items.title: Disk Space
items.text:
Each release requires disk space for:

  • Apps repository clones
  • Python virtual environment
  • Node modules
  • Built assets

Minimum recommended: 5 GB free space per site workspace

Actual usage depends on:

  • Number of apps installed
  • Number of releases retained (default: 7)
  • Backup retention policy

items.title: Network Requirements
items.text:
fmd needs network access to:

  • GitHub: Clone app repositories
  • PyPI: Install Python packages
  • npm registry: Install Node packages
  • Docker Hub/GHCR: Pull container images

If deploying behind a proxy, configure:

export HTTP_PROXY=http://proxy.example.com:8080
export HTTPS_PROXY=http://proxy.example.com:8080

items.title: Next Steps
items.text: Once you have all requirements installed, proceed to Installation.

items.title: Guides
items.text:
In-depth walkthroughs for everything fmd can do.

  • Deploy Modes

    Understand pull vs ship deployment strategies. Learn when to build on-server vs build locally and rsync.

  • Configuration

    Complete guide to the TOML configuration file. All options, defaults, and examples explained.

  • :lucide-github:{ .lg .middle }   GitHub Actions

    Automate deployments from CI/CD with the rtcamp/frappe-deployer GitHub Action. Pull and ship strategies.

  • Hooks & Lifecycle

    Customize every phase of the build and deployment process with shell hooks. Build assets, run tests, send notifications.

  • Frappe Cloud Sync

    Import app lists, Python dependencies, and database backups directly from Frappe Cloud. Perfect for migrations.

  • Maintenance Mode

    Control when maintenance mode activates. Bypass tokens for developer access during deployments.

  • Remote Workers

    Run background workers on separate servers. Sync releases across machines for distributed setups.

  • Monorepo Apps

    Deploy apps from subdirectories of monorepos. Symlink for efficient workspace management.

  • Rollback & Recovery

    Instant rollback to previous releases. Automatic rollback on migration failures. Backup and restore workflows.

items.title: Configuration
items.text: Complete reference for the fmd configuration file (site.toml).

items.title: Overview
items.text:
fmd uses TOML configuration files to define deployment settings. This eliminates the need for long command-line flags and makes configurations reusable and version-controllable.

Basic structure:

site_name = "mysite.localhost"
github_token = "ghp_xxx"

[[apps]]
repo = "frappe/frappe"
ref = "version-15"

[switch]
maintenance_mode = true
backups = true

See example-config.toml for complete documentation of all options.

items.title: Site and Bench
items.text:
site_name = "mysite.localhost"

The Frappe site name. This must match the site created in Frappe Manager.

bench_name = ""

The bench/container identifier. Defaults to site_name if not specified. Only set this if your bench directory name differs from your site name.

items.title: Apps Configuration
items.text: Define apps to install with [[apps]] sections. Each app is a separate block.

items.title: Basic App
items.text:
[[apps]]
repo = "frappe/frappe"
ref = "version-15"
  • repo: Repository in format "org/repo" or full GitHub URL
  • ref: Git branch, tag, or commit hash

items.title: Multiple Apps
items.text:
[[apps]]
repo = "frappe/frappe"
ref = "version-15"

[[apps]]
repo = "frappe/erpnext"
ref = "version-15"

[[apps]]
repo = "frappe/hrms"
ref = "main"

Apps are installed in the order specified.

items.title: Private Repositories
items.text:
github_token = "ghp_your_token_here"

[[apps]]
repo = "my-org/private-app"
ref = "main"

Set github_token at the top level to access private repositories.

items.title: Monorepo Apps
items.text:
For apps in subdirectories:

[[apps]]
repo = "my-org/monorepo"
ref = "main"
subdir_path = "apps/my-app"

See Monorepo Apps Guide for details.

items.title: Symlinked Apps (Development)
items.text:
[[apps]]
repo = "my-org/my-app"
ref = "develop"
symlink = true

Symlink instead of copying — useful for development where you want to edit code directly.

items.title: Clone Options
items.text:
[[apps]]
repo = "frappe/erpnext"
ref = "version-15"
shallow_clone = true      # Default: true
remote_name = "upstream"  # Default: "upstream"
  • shallow_clone: Use --depth=1 for faster cloning (saves bandwidth and disk)
  • remote_name: Git remote name

items.title: Release Configuration
items.text:
Control how releases are created and managed.

[release]
releases_retain_limit = 7
python_version = "3.11"
node_version = "20"

items.title: Retention Limit
items.text:
releases_retain_limit = 7

Number of releases to keep. Older releases are automatically deleted. Default: 7

items.title: Python and Node Versions
items.text:
python_version = "3.11"
node_version = "20"

Pin specific versions for your release. If not specified, uses system defaults.

items.title: Runner Mode
items.text:
mode = "exec"  # or "image"
  • exec (default): Run build in existing docker-compose containers
  • image: Run build in temporary containers (works without running services)

items.title: Platform (Cross-Architecture)
items.text:
platform = "linux/arm64"

Force a specific Docker platform. Useful for cross-architecture deployments (e.g., build on x86_64, deploy to ARM64).

items.title: Switch Configuration
items.text:
Control behavior during release switch (activation).

[switch]
migrate = true
migrate_timeout = 300
maintenance_mode = true
maintenance_mode_phases = ["migrate"]
backups = true
rollback = false
search_replace = true

items.title: Migration
items.text:
migrate = true
migrate_timeout = 300
  • migrate: Run bench migrate during switch
  • migrate_timeout: Timeout in seconds for migrations (default: 300)

items.title: Maintenance Mode
items.text:
maintenance_mode = true
maintenance_mode_phases = ["migrate"]
  • maintenance_mode: Enable maintenance mode during switch
  • maintenance_mode_phases: When to enable (options: "drain", "migrate")

See Maintenance Mode Guide for details.

items.title: Backups
items.text:
backups = true
backup_timeout = 600
  • backups: Take database backup before switch
  • backup_timeout: Timeout in seconds (default: 600)

items.title: Rollback
items.text:
rollback = false

Automatically rollback to previous release if switch fails. Default: false

Warning

Auto-rollback can hide issues. Better to fix problems and redeploy than silently rollback.

items.title: Search and Replace
items.text:
search_replace = true
search_replace_pairs = []

Run search-replace operations during switch. See Search-Replace Command.

items.title: Worker Draining
items.text:
drain_workers = false
drain_workers_timeout = 300
skip_stale_workers = true
skip_stale_timeout = 15
worker_kill_timeout = 15

Control background worker behavior during switch:

  • drain_workers: Wait for workers to finish jobs before switch
  • drain_workers_timeout: Max time to wait for workers (seconds)
  • skip_stale_workers: Skip workers that haven't processed jobs recently
  • skip_stale_timeout: Consider worker stale after N seconds of inactivity
  • worker_kill_timeout: Force-kill workers after this timeout

items.title: Frappe Cloud Integration
items.text:
Sync apps, dependencies, or database from Frappe Cloud.

[fc]
api_key = "fc_your_key"
api_secret = "fc_your_secret"
site_name = "yoursite.frappe.cloud"
team_name = "your-team"

[release]
use_fc_apps = true
use_fc_deps = true

[switch]
use_fc_db = true
  • use_fc_apps: Import app list and commit hashes from FC
  • use_fc_deps: Import Python version from FC
  • use_fc_db: Download and restore latest FC database backup

See Frappe Cloud Sync Guide for details.

items.title: Ship Mode (Remote Deployment)
items.text:
Configure remote server for ship mode deployments.

[ship]
server_ip = "192.168.1.100"
ssh_user = "frappe"
ssh_port = 22
remote_workspace_root = "/home/frappe/frappe/sites/mysite.localhost/workspace"

See Deploy Modes Guide for details.

items.title: Remote Workers
items.text:
Enable remote workers to run background jobs on separate servers.

[remote_worker]
server_ip = "192.168.1.200"
ssh_user = "frappe"
ssh_port = 22

See Remote Workers Guide for details.

items.title: Hooks
items.text: Customize build and deployment lifecycle with shell hooks.

items.title: App-Level Hooks
items.text:
[[apps]]
repo = "my-org/my-app"
ref = "main"

before_bench_build = """
npm ci
npm run build:prod
"""

after_bench_build = """
echo "Build complete for my-app"
"""

items.title: Global Hooks
items.text:
[release]
before_bench_build = """
echo "Starting build..."
"""

after_bench_build = """
echo "Build complete!"
"""

items.title: Host Hooks
items.text:
Prefix with host_ to run on host instead of inside container:

[[apps]]
repo = "my-org/my-app"
ref = "main"

host_after_bench_build = """
curl -X POST "$WEBHOOK_URL" -d "Build complete"
"""

items.title: Available Hooks
items.text:
Build phase (per-app and global):

  • before_bench_build
  • after_bench_build
  • before_python_install
  • after_python_install

Switch phase (global only):

  • before_restart
  • after_restart
  • host_before_restart
  • host_after_restart

See Hooks Guide for detailed examples.

items.title: Environment Variables in Hooks
items.text:
Hooks have access to these environment variables:

  • $SITE_NAME: Site name
  • $APP_NAME: Current app name (app-level hooks only)
  • $RELEASE_DIR: Release directory path
  • $WORKSPACE_ROOT: Workspace root path

Custom env vars via app_env:

[[apps]]
repo = "my-org/my-app"
ref = "main"
app_env = { API_KEY = "secret123", ENV = "production" }

items.title: Command-Line Overrides
items.text:
Most config file options can be overridden via CLI flags:

fmd deploy pull --config site.toml \
  --maintenance-mode \
  --python-version 3.12 \
  --app frappe/frappe:develop

CLI flags take precedence over config file values.

items.title: Multiple Environments
items.text:
Use separate config files for different environments:

.github/configs/
├── dev.toml
├── staging.toml
└── prod.toml

Then deploy with:

fmd deploy pull --config .github/configs/prod.toml

items.title: Validation
items.text:
fmd validates your config before deployment:

  • Required fields present
  • Valid TOML syntax
  • Reasonable timeout values
  • App format correctness

Validation errors are shown immediately — fmd won't start building with invalid config.

items.title: Next Steps
items.text:
• See example-config.toml for complete schema
  • Learn about hooks for customization
  • Set up GitHub Actions for CI/CD

items.title: Deploy Modes
items.text: fmd supports two deployment strategies: pull and ship. Each has different use cases and tradeoffs.

items.title: Overview
items.text: Mode Build Location Deploy Target When to Use pull Production server Same server Standard deployments, server has build capacity ship CI runner/local Remote via SSH Offload builds, cross-arch, multi-server deploy

items.title: Pull Mode
items.text: Build on the production server, deploy on the same server.

items.title: How It Works
items.text:
1. Connect to production server where Frappe Manager bench exists
  2. Run fmd deploy pull directly on the server
  3. fmd creates a new release by building inside Docker on that server
  4. Switch to new release atomically

items.title: Usage
items.text:
fmd deploy pull mysite.localhost \
  --app frappe/frappe:version-15 \
  --app frappe/erpnext:version-15 \
  --maintenance-mode --backups

Or with a config file:

fmd deploy pull --config site.toml

items.title: Advantages
items.text:
• Simple setup: No SSH configuration needed
  • Direct control: Run commands directly on production
  • Immediate feedback: See build logs in real-time
  • No file transfer: Everything happens locally

items.title: Disadvantages
items.text:
• Server resource usage: Build consumes CPU/RAM on production
  • Potential downtime: Heavy builds can affect running services
  • Sequential only: Can't deploy to multiple servers in parallel
  • Architecture-specific: Must build on same arch as production

items.title: When to Use Pull
items.text:
• Small to medium deployments
  • Server has enough CPU/RAM to build without affecting services
  • Single-server setup
  • You have direct SSH access to production

items.title: Example: Pull from CI
items.text:
Even in CI, you can SSH to production and run pull:

- name: Deploy via pull
  run: |
    ssh user@prod "cd /path/to/bench && \
      fmd deploy pull --config site.toml"

items.title: Ship Mode
items.text: Build locally or in CI, then ship to remote production server.

items.title: How It Works
items.text:
1. Build release in Docker on your local machine or CI runner
  2. Rsync the built release directory to production server
  3. SSH to production and switch to the new release

items.title: Usage
items.text:
Requires a [ship] section in your config:

[ship]
server_ip = "192.168.1.100"
ssh_user = "frappe"
ssh_port = 22
remote_workspace_root = "/home/frappe/frappe/sites/mysite.localhost/workspace"

Then deploy:

fmd deploy ship --config site.toml

items.title: Advantages
items.text:
• Offload builds: Free up production resources
  • Parallel deploys: Ship same release to multiple servers
  • Cross-architecture: Build on x86, deploy to ARM (or vice versa)
  • Faster deploys: Rsync is faster than rebuilding on each server
  • Pre-validated: Test release locally before shipping

items.title: Disadvantages
items.text:
• Complex setup: Requires SSH keys, rsync, proper permissions
  • Network dependency: Large file transfer over network
  • Initial slowness: First build downloads all dependencies
  • Storage overhead: Need disk space on both machines

items.title: When to Use Ship
items.text:
• Multiple production servers (deploy once, ship to many)
  • Limited production resources (small VPS, shared hosting)
  • Cross-architecture deployments
  • Want to test release locally before production
  • CI/CD pipeline with powerful runners

items.title: Example: Ship from GitHub Actions
items.text:
- name: Deploy via ship
  uses: rtcamp/frappe-deployer@main
  with:
    command: ship
    config_path: .github/configs/site.toml
    gh_token: ${{ secrets.GH_TOKEN }}
    ssh_private_key: ${{ secrets.SSH_PRIVATE_KEY }}

See GitHub Actions Guide for complete setup.

items.title: Hybrid Approach
items.text:
You can mix modes:

  • Development: Use pull mode on staging server
  • Production: Use ship mode for controlled releases

Or:

  • Regular deploys: Use pull for convenience
  • Emergency deploys: Use ship to build on powerful local machine

items.title: Build Time (Frappe + ERPNext, 4-core server)
items.text: Mode First Build Subsequent Notes pull ~8-12 min ~3-5 min Uses .cache on server ship ~10-15 min ~4-6 min Initial download, then rsync delta

items.title: Network Transfer (ship mode)
items.text:
Release Content Size Transfer Time (1 Gbps) Apps + venv ~500 MB ~4-5 seconds Node modules ~200 MB ~2 seconds Built assets ~50 MB <1 second Total ~750 MB ~7-10 seconds

Subsequent deploys transfer only changed files (much smaller).

items.title: Choosing a Mode
items.text:
Use this decision tree:

Do you have multiple production servers?
├─ YES → Use ship (build once, deploy to all)
└─ NO → Does production server have >4 CPU cores and >8 GB RAM?
    ├─ YES → Use pull (simpler)
    └─ NO → Use ship (offload builds)

items.title: Advanced: Custom Build Server
items.text:
You can designate a dedicated build server:

  1. Build server: Runs fmd in ship mode, builds releases
  2. Production servers: Receive releases via rsync, only run switch

This separates concerns:

  • Build server handles dependency downloads, compilations
  • Production servers only run application code

Example workflow:

# On build server
fmd deploy ship --config server1.toml
fmd deploy ship --config server2.toml
fmd deploy ship --config server3.toml

All three servers receive the same pre-built release.

items.title: Next Steps
items.text:
• Learn about GitHub Actions integration
  • Configure hooks to customize build process
  • Understand release lifecycle

items.title: GitHub Action
items.text: Automate Frappe deployments from GitHub Actions using two strategies: pull (remote server pulls and builds) and ship (CI runner builds, rsyncs to remote).

items.title: Strategies
items.text: Strategy Build location Deploy target When to use pull Remote server Same server Server has enough CPU/RAM to build; simpler setup ship CI runner (Docker) Remote server via rsync Offload build from server; cross-arch builds

items.title: pull
items.text:
The CI runner SSHes into the remote server and runs fmd deploy pull there. The server clones apps, builds the release, and switches — all on the server itself.

Required secrets/vars:

Name Type Description GH_TOKEN secret GitHub token for private repo access SSH_PRIVATE_KEY secret Private key for the remote server SSH_SERVER secret Remote server hostname or IP SSH_USER secret SSH username (e.g. frappe) SITE_NAME var Frappe site name (e.g. mysite.example.com) SSH_PORT var SSH port — optional, defaults to 22

Minimal workflow (.github/workflows/deploy-pull.yml):

name: Deploy (pull)

on:
  push:
    branches:
      - main
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Deploy via pull
        uses: rtcamp/frappe-deployer@main
        with:
          command: pull
          sitename: ${{ vars.SITE_NAME }}
          config_path: .github/configs/site.toml
          gh_token: ${{ secrets.GH_TOKEN }}
          ssh_private_key: ${{ secrets.SSH_PRIVATE_KEY }}
          ssh_server: ${{ secrets.SSH_SERVER }}
          ssh_user: ${{ secrets.SSH_USER }}

With all switch options:

      - name: Deploy via pull
        uses: rtcamp/frappe-deployer@main
        with:
          command: pull
          sitename: ${{ vars.SITE_NAME }}
          config_path: .github/configs/site.toml
          gh_token: ${{ secrets.GH_TOKEN }}
          ssh_private_key: ${{ secrets.SSH_PRIVATE_KEY }}
          ssh_server: ${{ secrets.SSH_SERVER }}
          ssh_user: ${{ secrets.SSH_USER }}
          ssh_port: ${{ vars.SSH_PORT || '22' }}
          migrate: "true"
          migrate_timeout: "300"
          drain_workers: "true"
          drain_workers_timeout: "300"
          skip_stale_workers: "true"
          maintenance_mode_phases: "migrate"
          worker_kill_timeout: "15"

items.title: ship
items.text:
The CI runner builds the release locally inside Docker (no Frappe Manager services needed), rsyncs it to the remote server, then SSHes in to run fmd release switch.

The config file must include a [ship] section pointing at the remote server:

[ship]
host = "192.168.1.100"
ssh_user = "frappe"
ssh_port = 22

Required secrets/vars: same as pull (GH_TOKEN, SSH_PRIVATE_KEY, SSH_SERVER, SSH_USER). No SITE_NAME — the site name comes from site_name in the TOML config.

Minimal workflow (.github/workflows/deploy-ship.yml):

name: Deploy (ship)

on:
  push:
    branches:
      - main
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Set up Docker Buildx
        uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v3

      - name: Deploy via ship
        uses: rtcamp/frappe-deployer@main
        with:
          command: ship
          config_path: .github/configs/site.toml
          gh_token: ${{ secrets.GH_TOKEN }}
          ssh_private_key: ${{ secrets.SSH_PRIVATE_KEY }}
          ssh_server: ${{ secrets.SSH_SERVER }}
          ssh_user: ${{ secrets.SSH_USER }}

With all switch options:

      - name: Deploy via ship
        uses: rtcamp/frappe-deployer@main
        with:
          command: ship
          config_path: .github/configs/site.toml
          gh_token: ${{ secrets.GH_TOKEN }}
          ssh_private_key: ${{ secrets.SSH_PRIVATE_KEY }}
          ssh_server: ${{ secrets.SSH_SERVER }}
          ssh_user: ${{ secrets.SSH_USER }}
          ssh_port: ${{ vars.SSH_PORT || '22' }}
          migrate: "true"
          migrate_timeout: "300"
          drain_workers: "true"
          drain_workers_timeout: "300"
          skip_stale_workers: "true"
          maintenance_mode_phases: "migrate"
          worker_kill_timeout: "15"
          runner_image: "ghcr.io/my-org/fm-builder:latest"

ship-only inputs:

Input Default Description runner_image auto-detected Docker image used to build the release. Override when you need a pinned or custom builder. existing_release — Skip the build step and deploy a release already present locally (format: release_YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS). skip_rsync false Skip the rsync step — useful when the release is already on the remote server.

items.title: Shared inputs
items.text:
Both pull and ship support the same switch-phase inputs:

Input Default Description migrate true Run bench migrate after switch migrate_timeout 300 Seconds before migrate is killed migrate_command — Override the migrate command entirely drain_workers false Gracefully drain background workers before restart drain_workers_timeout 300 Seconds to wait for workers to finish drain_workers_poll 5 Poll interval while draining (seconds) skip_stale_workers true Skip workers that haven't checked in recently skip_stale_timeout 15 Seconds before a worker is considered stale worker_kill_timeout 15 Seconds before force-killing workers worker_kill_poll 3.0 Poll interval while waiting to kill workers maintenance_mode_phases "" Space-separated phases to enable maintenance mode: drain, migrate additional_commands — Extra CLI flags appended to the fmd deploy invocation ssh_port 22 SSH port for the remote server config_path — Path to TOML config file, relative to repo root config_content — Inline TOML config string (alternative to config_path) app_env — Per-app env vars injected into build hooks — see Per-app environment

items.title: Per-app environment
items.text:
Inject secrets into app hook scripts without storing them in the config file:

      - name: Deploy
        uses: rtcamp/frappe-deployer@main
        with:
          command: pull   # or ship
          # ...
          app_env: |
            my-private-app:LICENSE_KEY=${{ secrets.MY_APP_LICENSE_KEY }}
            another-app:API_SECRET=${{ secrets.ANOTHER_API_SECRET }}

Format: one app-name:KEY=VALUE per line. The app-name prefix must match the Python module name of the app (last path segment of the repo, e.g. godam_core for my-org/custom-app). All declared vars are exported before any app's hook scripts run.

items.title: Hooks
items.text:
Each app can define up to 8 hooks in [[apps]]. The [switch] section has 4 more for restart-time.

Build-phase hooks (per-app, in [[apps]]):

Hook Runs in When before_bench_build container Before bench build for this app after_bench_build container After bench build for this app host_before_bench_build host Before bench build for this app host_after_bench_build host After bench build for this app before_python_install container Before uv pip install for this app after_python_install container After uv pip install for this app host_before_python_install host Before uv pip install for this app host_after_python_install host After uv pip install for this app

Switch-phase hooks (global, in [switch]):

Hook Runs in When before_restart container Before services restart after_restart container After services restart host_before_restart host Before services restart host_after_restart host After services restart

Hooks prefixed host_ run on the host machine. All others run inside the Docker container. Each hook value is either an inline shell script or a path to a .sh/.py file.

Example config with hooks:

[[apps]]
repo = "my-org/custom-app"
ref = "main"
before_bench_build = """
npm ci
npm run build:prod
"""
host_after_bench_build = """
curl -s -X POST "$WEBHOOK_URL" -d "app=$APP_NAME built"
"""

[switch]
host_before_restart = "echo 'switching to new release'"
after_restart = "bench --site $SITE_NAME clear-cache"

Hook values are always inline shell. If the value is a path ending in .sh or .py (or starts with /, ./, ~/), fmd reads that file and runs its contents — but the file must exist on the machine running the hook at deploy time (remote server for pull; CI runner for host_ hooks in ship). Repo-committed scripts don't automatically appear on the remote server, so inline scripts are the safest choice for cross-environment hooks.

Hook scripts receive these environment variables:

Variable Value BENCH_PATH Path to the frappe-bench symlink WORKSPACE_ROOT Path to the workspace root APPS Comma-separated list of installed app names SITE_NAME Frappe site name APP_NAME Current app name (build-phase hooks only) APP_PATH Path to the current app directory (build-phase hooks only)

Plus every field from the config as an uppercase env var (e.g. SITE_NAME, MIGRATE, PYTHON_VERSION).

For multiline values (e.g. a .env file), base64-encode the secret and pass via app_env:

          app_env: |
            my-app:DOTENV_B64=${{ secrets.MY_APP_DOTENV_B64 }}

Then decode inside the hook:

echo "${DOTENV_B64}" | base64 -d > /path/to/.env

items.title: Config file setup
items.text:
Both strategies load deployment configuration from a TOML file committed to the repo. Create .github/configs/site.toml:

For pull:

site_name = "mysite.example.com"

[[apps]]
repo = "frappe/frappe"
ref = "version-15"

[[apps]]
repo = "frappe/erpnext"
ref = "version-15"

[switch]
migrate = true
maintenance_mode = true
maintenance_mode_phases = ["migrate"]
backups = true
rollback = false

For ship (adds a [ship] section):

site_name = "mysite.example.com"

[[apps]]
repo = "frappe/frappe"
ref = "version-15"

[[apps]]
repo = "frappe/erpnext"
ref = "version-15"

[ship]
host = "192.168.1.100"
ssh_user = "frappe"
ssh_port = 22

[switch]
migrate = true
maintenance_mode = true
maintenance_mode_phases = ["migrate"]
backups = true
rollback = false

See example-config.toml for the full schema.

items.title: Setting up secrets
items.text:
In your GitHub repository: Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions

Name Where Value GH_TOKEN Secrets GitHub PAT with repo scope (for private repos) or GITHUB_TOKEN for public SSH_PRIVATE_KEY Secrets Contents of the private key file (e.g. cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519) SSH_SERVER Secrets Remote server IP or hostname SSH_USER Secrets SSH username on the remote server SITE_NAME Variables Frappe site name — pull only SSH_PORT Variables SSH port if not 22 — optional

Generate a dedicated deploy key:

ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "github-actions-deploy" -f ~/.ssh/deploy_key -N ""
cat ~/.ssh/deploy_key        # → paste as SSH_PRIVATE_KEY secret
cat ~/.ssh/deploy_key.pub    # → append to ~/.ssh/authorized_keys on remote server

items.title: Reference
items.text:
Technical reference documentation for fmd internals and advanced topics.

  • Concepts

    Understand release lifecycle, atomic deployments, workspace structure, and the core concepts behind fmd.

  • Architecture

    Deep dive into runner system, mixins, hook lifecycle, and internal implementation details.

  • Directory Structure

    Complete breakdown of workspace layout, release directories, symlinks, and persistent data organization.

  • Troubleshooting

    Common issues and solutions. Debug build failures, migration errors, network problems, and more.

items.title: Deployment Modes
items.text: Two user-facing deploy commands (pull, ship) built on three internal managers.

items.title: Git-based (User-Facing)
items.text: Mode Build happens Deploy happens Transport CLI Command pull Server Server SSH / direct fmd deploy pull ship Runner (local/CI) Server rsync → DOCKER_HOST SSH fmd deploy ship

items.title: pull
items.text: Everything runs on the server. Clone apps from git, pip install, bench build, fmx restart — all in-place. Handled by PullManager.

items.title: ship
items.text: Runner creates a fully baked release directory locally (inside Docker containers), rsyncs it to the server, then finalizes remotely (bench symlink, fmx restart, app install) via DOCKER_HOST=ssh://. Server never touches git. Handled by ShipManager(BakeManager).

items.title: Image-based (Internal Managers)
items.text:
Manager Purpose Status BakeManager Build Docker images from source ✅ exists (used internally by ship) PublishManager Deploy via image registry 🔲 future

Note: BakeManager is an internal manager used by ship mode. There is no fmd deploy bake or fmd bake command exposed to users.

items.title: Implementation status
items.text: Mode/Manager Status Class pull ✅ CLI command PullManager ship ✅ CLI command ShipManager BakeManager ✅ internal only BakeManager PublishManager 🔲 future —

items.title: Runner system
items.text:
All managers use a runner + host_runner pair. runner handles container/remote commands. host_runner handles local host commands (hooks, scripts).

CommandRunner (ABC)
├── FMRunner        — compose.exec into running FM frappe service
├── HostRunner      — run_command_with_exit_code on local host
└── BuildRunner     — two sub-paths based on config.build_frappe:
      ├── build_frappe set   → DockerClient().run(builder_image, volume=bench)
      └── build_frappe None  → compose.exec(service="frappe")

FMRunner is used by PullManager for all container commands. BuildRunner is used by BakeManager/ShipManager — it knows whether to spin up an ephemeral builder container or exec into a running compose service.

items.title: BuildRunner sub-path decision
items.text:
config.build_frappe present?

YES → "offline image build"
      DockerClient().run(image=builder_image_name, volume=self.path:/workspace/frappe-bench)
      - Ephemeral container per command (rm=True)
      - No compose stack needed
      - Workdir: /workspace/frappe-bench (hardcoded)

NO  → "live FM deployment"
      compose_project.docker.compose.exec(service="frappe")
      - Execs into already-running compose frappe service
      - Requires FM stack to be up
      - Workdir: /workspace/{bench_dir.name}

items.title: Mixin system
items.text:
Shared bench operations live in ops/ mixins. All mixins assume self.runner, self.host_runner, self.printer, self.config, self.apps, self.bench_cli, self.fmx, self.bench_path, self.current.

BenchMixin        — python_env_create, bench_setup_requiments, bench_build,
                    bench_restart, bench_symlink, configure_uv,
                    bench_install_all_apps_in_python_env,
                    _run_script, get_script_env
AppsMixin         — clone_apps, bench_install_apps
BackupMixin       — bench_db_and_configs_backup, bench_backup, bench_restore
SymlinksMixin     — configure_symlinks
CleanupMixin      — cleanup_releases, cleanup_backups

items.title: Class hierarchy
items.text:
PullManager(BackupMixin, AppsMixin, BenchMixin, SymlinksMixin, CleanupMixin)
  runner      = FMRunner
  host_runner = HostRunner

BakeManager(BenchMixin)
  runner      = BuildRunner
  host_runner = HostRunner
  bench_build — overrides BenchMixin (different flags: --hard-link, iterates bench_directory.apps)
  clone_apps  — overrides AppsMixin (parallel cloning, data_directory param)

ShipManager(BakeManager)
  runner        = BuildRunner       (local bake phase)
  host_runner   = HostRunner        (local host hooks)
  remote_runner = FMRunner          (remote finalization, DOCKER_HOST=ssh://user@server)

items.title: bench_build divergence
items.text:
BenchMixin.bench_build and BakeManager.bench_build are intentionally kept separate.

BenchMixin (pull mode) BakeManager (bake/ship mode) Build flag --production --hard-link App iteration self.apps config list (uses app.app_name) bench_directory.apps dirs (resolves app_config by dir_name lookup) Context Live FM deployment Offline image build / ship

items.title: Hook system
items.text: 12 lifecycle hooks across 3 phases. Hooks run as shell scripts. Values can be inline script content or a path to a .sh/.py file.

items.title: Phases
items.text: Phase Container hooks Host hooks python_install before_python_install, after_python_install host_before_python_install, host_after_python_install bench_build before_bench_build, after_bench_build host_before_bench_build, host_after_bench_build restart before_restart, after_restart host_before_restart, host_after_restart

items.title: Scope
items.text:
• restart hooks — global config level only
  • python_install hooks — per-app config (with global fallback)
  • bench_build hooks — per-app config (with global fallback)

items.title: Execution
items.text:
• Container hooks → _run_script(..., container=True) → runs inside frappe container
  • Host hooks → _run_script(..., container=False) → runs on host via HostRunner
  • Per-app hooks get APP_NAME and APP_PATH in env
  • All hooks get full config env via get_script_env()

items.title: ship mode — detailed flow
items.text:
Local runner
  ShipManager.ship()
    1. bake phase (inherited from BakeManager)
       ├── clone_apps(current)
       ├── chown_dir(...)
       ├── python_env_create(current)
       ├── bench_setup_requiments(current)
       ├── sync_configs_with_files(current)
       └── bench_build(current)

    2. rsync phase
       └── rsync -az --delete release_dir/ user@server:remote_path/release_dir/

    3. remote finalization (via DOCKER_HOST=ssh://user@server)
       ├── bench_symlink(remote_bench_dir)
       ├── remote_runner.restart_services(args, remote_bench_dir)   ← fmx restart
       └── bench_install_apps(remote_bench_dir)

items.title: Config (ship section in config.toml)
items.text:
[ship]
host = "user@server"           # SSH target
remote_path = "/path/on/server"  # deploy path on server
rsync_options = []             # extra rsync flags (optional)

items.title: Files
items.text: File Purpose ship_manager.py ShipManager(BakeManager) config/ship.py ShipConfig pydantic model config/config.py Add ship: Optional[ShipConfig] commands/ship.py fmd ship CLI command

items.title: Phase 1 — Eliminate duplication
items.text:
1. runner/build.py — new BuildRunner(CommandRunner) with build_frappe branching
  2. runner/__init__.py — export BuildRunner
  3. ops/bench.py — move real _run_script + get_script_env implementations into BenchMixin (merged from both managers)
  4. bake_manager.py — extend BenchMixin, add self.runner = BuildRunner(...) + self.host_runner = HostRunner(...), delete ~600 lines of duplicated ops
  5. pull_manager.py — delete _run_script + get_script_env (now inherited from BenchMixin)
  6. helpers.py — add log_execution_time + extract_timestamp (currently duplicated in both managers)

Deleted from BakeManager (replaced by BenchMixin): _run_script, get_script_env, configure_uv, python_env_create, bench_install_all_apps_in_python_env, bench_setup_requiments, bench_restart, bench_symlink, is_app_installed_in_site

Kept in BakeManager (intentionally different): bench_build, clone_apps, chown_dir, setup_nvm_and_yarn, sync_configs_with_files, bake, is_baked, build_images, _build_frappe_base_image, _build_frappe_image, _build_nginx_image

items.title: Phase 2 — ship mode
items.text:
1. config/ship.py — ShipConfig(BaseModel) with host, remote_path, rsync_options
  2. config/config.py — add ship: Optional[ShipConfig] = None
  3. ship_manager.py — ShipManager(BakeManager) with rsync_release() + finalize_remote()
  4. commands/ship.py — fmd ship CLI command

items.title: Phase 3 — publish mode (future)
items.text: 1. After bake, SSH to server via DOCKER_HOST, swap compose service image, restart.

items.title: Deploy Modes
items.text:
fmd supports two deployment modes optimized for different workflows:

Mode Purpose Build Location Deploy Target pull Standard deployment On-server in docker-compose Same server ship Remote deployment Local machine Remote server via rsync

items.title: pull — Standard Deployment
items.text:
fmd deploy pull site.localhost --app frappe/frappe:version-15

Workflow: configure (if needed) → create release → switch

Where it runs: Directly on the target server using Frappe Manager's docker-compose services (MariaDB, Redis running).

Best for: Development, staging, and production servers where you deploy directly.

items.title: ship — Remote Deployment
items.text:
fmd deploy ship --config site.toml  # Builds locally, deploys remotely

Workflow: 1. Create release on local machine (uses fresh Docker container, no live services needed) 2. rsync release directory to remote server 3. SSH to remote, run fmd release switch to activate

Best for: Deploying from a local dev machine to a remote production server.

items.title: 1. configure (One-Time Setup)
items.text:
fmd release configure site.localhost

Converts a plain bench directory into a versioned release structure: - Creates deployment-data/ for persistent files (sites, config, logs) - Creates deployment-backup/ for backups - Initializes first release directory with timestamp

items.title: 2. create (Build New Release)
items.text:
fmd release create site.localhost

Creates a new timestamped release (release_YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS/): - Clones app repositories - Builds Python venv with release-scoped .uv/ cache - Installs Node.js in release-scoped .fnm/ - Runs bench build for frontend assets - Executes pre/post-build hooks

Safe: Does not touch the live bench symlink. Can run while site is serving traffic.

items.title: 3. switch (Activate Release)
items.text:
fmd release switch site.localhost release_20250410_120000

Atomically activates a release: - Drains workers (optional: drain_workers = true) - Takes DB backup (optional: backups = true) - Enables maintenance mode (during migrate phase only by default) - Symlinks workspace/frappe-bench → release_YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS/ - Runs bench migrate - Restarts services - Disables maintenance mode

Fast: Typically <30s downtime (just migrate + restart). Symlink switch is atomic.

items.title: 4. rollback
items.text:
fmd release switch site.localhost release_20250409_100000  # Previous release

Rollback is just another switch to an older release. Previous releases are preserved based on releases_retain_limit.

items.title: Directory Structure
items.text:
~/frappe/sites/site.localhost/
├── workspace/
│   ├── frappe-bench → release_20250410_120000  (symlink — atomic switch)
│   │
│   ├── deployment-data/                         (persistent across releases)
│   │   ├── sites/
│   │   │   ├── common_site_config.json
│   │   │   └── site.localhost/
│   │   │       ├── site_config.json
│   │   │       ├── private/
│   │   │       └── public/
│   │   ├── config/           (supervisor configs)
│   │   ├── logs/             (application logs)
│   │   └── apps/             (symlinked app clones, organized by release)
│   │       └── release_20250410_120000/
│   │           └── erpnext_clone/  (git clone of erpnext, symlinked)
│   │
│   ├── release_20250410_120000/    (current release)
│   │   ├── apps/
│   │   │   ├── frappe/             (full clone or copy)
│   │   │   └── erpnext → ../../deployment-data/apps/.../erpnext_clone  (symlink if enabled)
│   │   ├── env/                    (Python venv)
│   │   ├── .uv/                    (UV package cache, per-release)
│   │   ├── .fnm/                   (Node.js runtime, per-release)
│   │   ├── sites → ../deployment-data/sites  (symlink)
│   │   ├── config → ../deployment-data/config
│   │   ├── logs → ../deployment-data/logs
│   │   └── .fmd.toml               (config snapshot for this release)
│   │
│   ├── release_20250409_100000/    (previous release, kept for rollback)
│   └── .cache/                     (workspace-level caches)
│
└── deployment-backup/
    ├── release_20250410_120000/
    │   └── site.localhost_20250410_120030.sql.gz  (DB backup)
    └── release_20250409_100000/
        └── site.localhost_20250409_100030.sql.gz

items.title: Key Directories
items.text:
• deployment-data/: Survives across all releases. Contains site DB, files, and configs.
  • deployment-backup/: DB backups organized by release, with retention policy.
  • release_YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS/: Each release is fully isolated with its own Python venv, Node.js runtime, and app code.
  • frappe-bench symlink: Points to current active release. Switched atomically during deploy.

items.title: Runtime Isolation
items.text:
Each release has isolated Python and Node.js runtimes:

  • .uv/: UV package cache (per-release). Python packages installed here don't affect other releases.
  • .fnm/: Node.js installation (per-release). Multiple releases can use different Node versions.
  • env/: Python virtual environment (per-release). Different Python versions per release.

This allows: - Test a release with Python 3.11 while production runs Python 3.10 - Different Node.js versions for frontend builds - Instant rollback without dependency conflicts

items.title: Configuration Hierarchy
items.text:
Settings are merged from multiple sources (highest to lowest priority):

  1. CLI arguments — e.g., --app frappe/frappe:version-15 --maintenance-mode
  2. Environment variables — e.g., GITHUB_TOKEN=ghp_xxx
  3. TOML config file — site.toml loaded via --config or auto-discovered
  4. Default values — Built-in defaults from Pydantic models

Example: 

# Config file says: maintenance_mode = false
# CLI override: --maintenance-mode
# Result: maintenance enabled (CLI wins)
fmd deploy pull --config site.toml --maintenance-mode

items.title: Repository Format
items.text:
Apps are specified as org/repo:ref or org/repo:ref:subdir_path:

[[apps]]
repo = "frappe/frappe"
ref = "version-15"  # Branch, tag, or commit hash

[[apps]]
repo = "my-org/monorepo"
ref = "main"
subdir_path = "apps/custom-app"  # For monorepos
symlink = true                   # Symlink instead of copy

items.title: Build Process
items.text:
During release create:

  1. Clone: git clone https://github.com/org/repo (or use GitHub token for private)
  2. Checkout: git checkout ref
  3. Subdir: If subdir_path set, use that directory (monorepo support)
  4. Symlink: If symlink = true, symlink to deployment-data/apps/<release>/<app>_clone/
  5. Hooks: Run before_python_install → pip install → after_python_install
  6. Build: Run before_bench_build → bench build → after_bench_build
  7. Migrate: (During switch phase) bench migrate

items.title: Symlink vs Copy
items.text:
Copy (default): - App code is duplicated in each release - Slower clones, more disk space - Safer: each release is fully independent

Symlink (symlink = true): - App code is cloned once to deployment-data/apps/<release>/<app>_clone/ - Each release symlinks to the same clone - Faster deploys, less disk space - Recommended for monorepo subdirectory apps

items.title: Automatic Backups
items.text:
Before each release switch (if backups = true):

  1. Database dump: bench --site <site> backup --with-files (compressed .sql.gz)
  2. Storage: Saved to deployment-backup/release_YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS/
  3. Retention: Keeps last N backups based on releases_retain_limit

items.title: Retention Policy
items.text:
[release]
releases_retain_limit = 7  # Keep last 7 releases + their backups

When exceeded, fmd cleanup removes: - Oldest release directories - Corresponding backup directories

Manual cleanup: 

fmd cleanup site.localhost --release-retain-limit 3 --backup-retain-limit 5 --yes

items.title: How It Works
items.text: During release switch, fmd passes --maintenance-mode <phase> flags to fmx restart, which handles enabling and disabling the maintenance page. fmd itself has no standalone maintenance command — the behavior is entirely delegated to Frappe Manager's restart cycle.

items.title: Phase Control
items.text:
[switch]
maintenance_mode = true
maintenance_mode_phases = ["migrate"]  # Valid: "drain", "migrate"
  • ["migrate"] — Maintenance only during the DB migration step (default)
  • ["drain", "migrate"] — Maintenance during worker drain + migration
  • [] — Disable maintenance mode entirely (even if maintenance_mode = true)

The --maintenance-mode flag is passed once per phase, so ["drain", "migrate"] passes --maintenance-mode drain --maintenance-mode migrate to fmx restart.

items.title: Worker Draining
items.text:
Gracefully stop background workers before restart:

[switch]
drain_workers = true
drain_workers_timeout = 300    # Wait up to 5 min for jobs to finish
skip_stale_workers = true      # Skip workers that haven't checked in recently
skip_stale_timeout = 15        # Worker is stale if no checkin for 15s
worker_kill_timeout = 15       # Force-kill after 15s if drain fails

Drain workflow: 1. fmd marks workers for drain (background job enqueue is disabled) 2. Waits for workers to finish current jobs (up to drain_workers_timeout) 3. Skips stale workers (haven't checked in for skip_stale_timeout) 4. Force-kills workers still running after worker_kill_timeout

items.title: Frappe Cloud Integration
items.text:
Sync app lists, Python dependencies, or database backups from Frappe Cloud:

[release]
use_fc_apps = true   # Merge FC app list with local [[apps]]
use_fc_deps = true   # Set python_version from FC dependencies

[switch]
use_fc_db = true     # Download and restore latest FC backup at switch time

[fc]
api_key = "fc_xxx"
api_secret = "fc_xxx"
site_name = "mysite.frappe.cloud"
team_name = "my-team"

items.title: App Merge Behavior
items.text:
When use_fc_apps = true, fmd: 1. Fetches app list from Frappe Cloud API 2. Merges with local [[apps]] by repo name (case-insensitive) 3. FC commit hash overrides local ref (FC always has the deployed hash) 4. Preserves local settings: hooks, symlink, subdir_path from local config 5. Keeps local-only apps: Apps in local config but not in FC are preserved

Example: 

# Local config
[[apps]]
repo = "frappe/erpnext"
ref = "version-15"    # Branch name
symlink = true
before_bench_build = "echo 'Building ERPNext'"

# FC returns: repo="frappe/erpnext", ref="a1b2c3d4" (commit hash)
# Result: ref="a1b2c3d4" (FC commit), symlink=true, hook preserved
